elements.sent-2411-002: Teule \def\titlerunning{Functional Array Programming in an Extended Pi-Calculus.}
elements.sent-2411-003: We study the data-parallel language \butf, inspired by the \futhark language for array programming.
elements.sent-2411-004: We give a translation of \butf into a version of the \pic with broadcasting and labeled names.
elements.sent-2411-005: The translation is both complete and sound.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, we propose a cost model by annotating translated \butf processes.
elements.sent-2411-007: This is used for a complexity analysis of the translation.
elements.sent-2411-008: The \futhark programming language is a functional language whose goal is to abstract parallel array operations by means of utilizing second order array combinators, such as \map and \reduce \cite{FutharkPhd .}
elements.sent-2411-009: The \futhark compiler then efficiently translates code into optimized code for the targeted hardware.
elements.sent-2411-010: Parallel hardware, such as \glspl{GPU , does not support arbitrary nesting of parallel operations, nor arbitrarily large problem sizes, and the \futhark compiler therefore produces a program for which the outermost levels of nested operations of a program are executed in parallel.}
elements.sent-2411-011: The \gls{GPU programs produced by the \futhark compiler are therefore limited by the physical constraints of the hardware in question, and it would thus be interesting to analyze \futhark programs in the setting of an underlying parallel language without these limitations.}
elements.sent-2411-012: It is known that there exist sound translations of the $\lambda$-calculus and different reduction strategies into the simple $\pi$-calculus \cite{DBLP:books/daglib/0098267,DBLP:books/daglib/0004377 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Milner was the first to provide such a translation \cite{pi_functions_as_processes and Sangiorgi extended his work \cite{DBLP:conf/mfps/Sangiorgi93,DBLP:journals/iandc/Sangiorgi94,DBLP:journals/mscs/Sangiorgi99 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: These encodings identify the essence of how to implement a functional programming language on a parallel architecture using references in the form of name-passing and the ability to express arbitrary levels of nested concurrency and parallelism.
elements.sent-2411-015: In this paper we use this work as the inspiration for a translation of a functional array programming language which is a subset of \futhark into an extended $\pi$-calculus, $E\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-016: In $E\pi$ we extend the setting to one containing structured data \cite{pi_mobile_values_secure_communication,DBLP:conf/lics/BengtsonJPV09 and broadcasting, as these are central to the protocol used by \futhark.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Our focus is on how to encode the array structure and a subset of second-order array operators from \futhark into $E\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-018: For the proof of operational correspondence we use a coinductive approach which lends itself well to expressing the correctness of our encoding.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our approach is inspired by that of Amadio et al. \cite{DBLP:conf/fct/AmadioLT95 in that we distinguish between the ``important'' and ``administrative'' computation steps.}
elements.sent-2411-020: This also allows us to compare the cost of the translation to that of \futhark constructs.
elements.sent-2411-021: First we introduce \butf and the process calculus \epic that will be the target language of our translation.
elements.sent-2411-022: Basic Untyped \futhark (\butf) deals only with functional array computation and omits the module system of \futhark.
elements.sent-2411-023: The formation rules of \butf expressions are shown below. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} e ::= \ b\mid x\mid [e_1,\dots,e_n]\mid e_1[e_2]\mid\lambda x.e_1\mid e_1\ e_2\mid (e_1,\dots,e_n) \mid if\ e_1\ then\ e_2\ else\ e_3 b ::= n\mid map\midiota\midsize \mid\odot \end{aligned} \label{eq:syntax}\end{equation} \butf makes use of prefix application $e_1\ e_2$.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation} e ::= \ b\mid x\mid [e_1,\dots,e_n]\mid e_1[e_2]\mid\lambda x.e_1\mid e_1\ e_2\mid (e_1,\dots,e_n) \mid if\ e_1\ then\ e_2\ else\ e_3 b ::= n\mid map\midiota\midsize \mid\odot  \label{eq:syntax}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-024: Constants are denoted as $b$, and are integer constants, arithmetic operations $\odot$ and the array operations described in \cref{sec:arrayop .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Arrays are denoted by $[e_1,\dots,e_n]$ and tuples are denoted by $(e_1,\dots,e_n)$.
elements.sent-2411-026: The expression $e_1[e_2]$ will evaluate to the place in the array $e_1$ whose index is the value of $e_2$.
elements.sent-2411-027: To express a unary tuple, we use the notation $(x,)$, while a empty tuple is denoted as $()$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Fully evaluated expressions can be indexes with the index operator. \begin{equation}\label{eq:array_semantics} \frac{e_i\mra e_i' \quad 1 \leq i \leq n}{[e_1,\dots,e_i,\dots,e_n]\mra[e_1,\dots,e_i',\dots,e_n]} \frac{0\leq i\leq n-1}{[v_1,\dots,v_n][i]\mra v_{i+1}}\end{equation} Lastly, we have the conditional structure that allows branching depending on the result of $e_1$. \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \begin{prooftree} \hypo{v\neq 0} \infer1{if\ \mit{v}\ then\ e_2\ else\ e_3\rightarrow e_2} \end{prooftree}\qquad \begin{prooftree} \hypo{v = 0} \infer1{if\ \mit{v}\ then\ e_2\ else\ e_3\rightarrow e_3} \end{prooftree} \end{gathered}\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-029: These have been chosen since they can be used to define other common array operators such as concat, reduce, and scan \cite{p10 .}
elements.sent-2411-030: This allows us to simplify the translation and the proof of its correctness.

elements.sent-2411-032: The \map function allows for applying a function to each element in an array.
elements.sent-2411-033: The reduction rules for the function constants are shown below.

elements.sent-2411-036: The language used as the target for the translation is the Extended $\pi$-calculus (\epic), presented in previous work \cite{p10 , and is based on the \apic presented by Abadi, Blanchet, and Fournet \cite{DBLP:journals/jacm/AbadiBF18 .}}
elements.sent-2411-037: This calculus is extended with broadcast communication as presented by Hüttel and Pratas \cite{DBLP:journals/corr/HuttelP16 as well as simple first order composite names based on \cite{composite_names .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Processes are given by the formation rules below. \begin{equation}\label{fig:Process_pi_syntax} \begin{aligned} P ::= \pizero \mid P|Q \mid {!}P\mid\nu a.P \mid A.P\mid\bullet P \mid \ite{M}{N}{P}{Q} \qquad A::= \pisend c{\vec T}\mid \pirecv c{\Vec x}\mid\pibroad c{\vec T} c ::= a\mid x\mid a\cdot I\mid x\cdot I I::= n\mid x\midall\midtup\midlen T ::= n \mid a\mid x\mid T\odot T \end{aligned}\end{equation} $T$ ranges over terms that can be sent on channels.

elements.sent-2411-043: Processes $P$ can be the empty process $\pizero$ which cannot reduce further, parallel composition ($P \mid Q$) consisting of two processes in parallel, replication (${! {P}$) which constructs an unbounded number of process $P$ in parallel, and declaration of new names ($\nu u.P$), which restricts $u$ to the scope of $P$.}

elements.sent-2411-049: The process $\bullet P$ denotes that $P$ begins with an important computation step; this is explained in Section \ref{subsec:semantics and is used in analyzing the complexity of our encoding.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Broadcasting in \epic is denoted as $\pibroad c{\vec T}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: It can send a vector of terms $\vec{T}$ over a channel $c$ to multiple processes in a single reduction, atomically.
elements.sent-2411-052: A channel name $c$ can be a name ($a$) is a composite name consisting of a name followed by an identifier $I$ that can be either a number or a label.
elements.sent-2411-053: These labels are used to distinguish between several different translation constructs.
elements.sent-2411-054: In particular, in the encoding, labels describe if a reduction involves an entire array (all), the reduction of a tuple (tup) or the computation of the length of an array (len).
elements.sent-2411-055: The structure of the semantics for \epic is similar to that of the \pic, using a structural congruence relation that identifies process expression with the same structure and a reduction relation.

elements.sent-2411-073: The translation from \butf into the extended \pic is very similar to the approach of Robin Milner \cite{pi_functions_as_processes .}

elements.sent-2411-075: Our translation differs in that \butf uses not numbers but also arrays and the accompanying operators as values.
elements.sent-2411-076: First, we define the translation of the part of \butf\ that corresponds to an applied $\lambda$-calculus -- numbers, functions, and application, shown in \cref{eq:translate_normal_stuff .}

elements.sent-2411-088: This section will cover how arrays can be represented in \epic, and how this is used to translate \butf arrays.
elements.sent-2411-089: This approach represents each array element with a independent cell, which users communicate with.
elements.sent-2411-090: Here the extensions in \epic are very useful, because they allow addressing individual array cells, or all at once.
elements.sent-2411-091: We have decided to represent arrays as a replicated process listening on some handle, much like how functions are represented in the \pic.
elements.sent-2411-092: An array element is described by a cell process that listens on a broadcast for a request for all elements and listens on the composed name $\mit{handle}\cdot index$ for a request for a specific element. \begin{equation}\mit{Cell}(\mit{handle},\mit{index},\mit{value}) = {!}\pirecv {\mit{handle}\cdotall}r.\pisend r{\mit{index, value}}\mid{!}\pisend{\mit{handle}\cdot\mit{index}}{\mit{index}, \mit{value}}\end{equation} An array is a parallel composition of cells together with a single replicated sender that provides users of the array with its length.

elements.sent-2411-108: To be able to analyze the complexity and thus allowing us to reason about the translation, an annotated step notation is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-109: This is inspired by the tick-notation used in \cite{TypesForComplexityOfParallelComputationInPiCalculus .}
elements.sent-2411-110: Here, the $\bullet$ notation marks the important transitions in \epic that match a transition in \butf.
elements.sent-2411-111: In the translation we consider four different kinds of channels: outputs ($o \in \Omega$), handles ($h \in \Lambda$), signals ($d \in \Delta$), and collections ($c \in \Psi$).

elements.sent-2411-266: To compare the work (W) and span (S) with those of Futhark we carry out an analysis on the translation of \butf into \epic.
elements.sent-2411-267: We define work as the actual instructions that happen and span as the depth of parallel instructions.
elements.sent-2411-268: Our cost model is based on the number of $\bullet$-marked reductions encountered which were placed earlier to facilitate operational correspondence.

elements.sent-2411-272: In our comparison, work and span costs in \futhark are taken from the \futhark website\cite{FutharkWorkSpanModel .}
elements.sent-2411-273: The notion of span is the more interesting of the two, given the potential for parallelization in \epic.
elements.sent-2411-274: The first thing to note is that the values in \futhark have a cost and span of $\mathcal O(1)$, compared to the $\mathcal O(0)$ in the translation, which could indicate an unacknowledged cost in the translation.

elements.sent-2411-282: In this paper we have presented the \butf language, a $\lambda$-calculus with parallel arrays inspired by the \futhark programming language, and we show a translation of \butf into \epic, a variant of \pic that uses polyadic communication and broadcast.
elements.sent-2411-283: Our translation extends the translation from the $\lambda$-calculus to the \pic due to Milner et al. with the notion of arrays and involves defining the usual operations on arrays in a process calculus setting.
elements.sent-2411-284: Our proof of correctness uses a coinductively defined notion of operational correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-285: While we proof that the translation is correct in regards to operational correspondence, we do not show that the translation is fully abstract, or that translated programs diverge.
elements.sent-2411-286: We present a cost model for our version of the \pic in the form of a classification of reductions -- they can be either important or administrative.
elements.sent-2411-287: A cost analysis was performed for the translation to \epic, and its results were compared with the cost for \futhark's language constructs.
elements.sent-2411-288: This comparison shows that the map and reduce operations in \futhark are similar to the fully parallel ones shown here.
elements.sent-2411-289: Having broadcast in \epic makes it rather simple to implement array indexing.
elements.sent-2411-290: It would be interesting to consider an array structure without the use of broadcast.
elements.sent-2411-291: Here, one must take into account the result due to Ene and Muntean \cite{DBLP:conf/fct/EneM99 that broadcast communication is more expressive than point-to-point communication.}
elements.sent-2411-292: Our translation is not typed; the next step will be to introduce a type system in \butf and \epic, and extend the translation to also translate types.
elements.sent-2411-293: Binary session types \cite{gay2005subtyping would be a natural candidate to ensure that the channels in the translation follow a particular protocol.}
elements.sent-2411-294: Furthermore, it is of interest to validate if the translation can be done in the standard \pic without broadcast and composite names.
elements.sent-2411-295: This would make it possible to relate the translation with other work in the \pic domain.
elements.sent-2411-296: Broadcasting and composed names as primitives in \epic might also be unrealistic, when considering \epic as an abstraction for real world hardware.. \bibliographystyle{eptcs.}
